quiz_questions.question_text: What is electric current?

options.text: Resistance times voltage
Charge times time
Voltage divided by power
Rate of flow of charge: I = Q/t

quiz_questions.explanation: Current is the rate of flow of charge: I = Q/t, measured in amperes.

quiz_questions.question_text: State Ohm's Law.

options.text: V = I²R
V = IR
V = IR²
V = I/R

quiz_questions.explanation: Ohm's Law: V = IR. Voltage equals current times resistance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of resistance?

options.text: Volt (V)
Ampere (A)
Ohm (Ω)
Watt (W)

quiz_questions.explanation: The SI unit of resistance is the ohm (Ω). 1 Ω = 1 V/A.

quiz_questions.question_text: Conventional current flows from:

options.text: Negative to positive
It doesn't flow
Positive to negative
Both directions equally

quiz_questions.explanation: Conventional current flows from positive to negative, opposite to actual electron flow.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for electrical power?

options.text: P = I/V
P = IV
P = IR
P = V/I

quiz_questions.explanation: Power P = IV = I²R = V²/R.

quiz_questions.question_text: What factors affect resistance of a wire?

options.text: Voltage, current, and the power supply used
Material, length, and cross-sectional area
Temperature, color, and surface finish only
Current direction and the electron charge

quiz_questions.explanation: Resistance depends on material (resistivity), length, and cross-sectional area: R = ρL/A.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a non-ohmic device?

options.text: Any battery
One that follows Ohm's Law
A perfect conductor
One whose resistance changes with current (e.g., diode)

quiz_questions.explanation: Non-ohmic devices have resistance that changes with current or voltage, like diodes and filament bulbs.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of current?

options.text: The volt (V)
The ohm (Ω)
The ampere (A)
The watt (W)

quiz_questions.explanation: The SI unit of electric current is the ampere (A). One ampere equals one coulomb of charge flowing per second.

quiz_questions.question_text: If a wire's length is doubled, what happens to its resistance?

options.text: Resistance is halved
Resistance stays the same
Resistance doubles
Resistance quadruples

quiz_questions.explanation: R = ρL/A. Since R is directly proportional to length L, doubling L doubles R.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for resistance in terms of resistivity?

options.text: R = ρA/L
R = ρL/A
R = ρ/(LA)
R = L/(ρA)

quiz_questions.explanation: R = ρL/A, where ρ is resistivity, L is length, and A is cross-sectional area.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'resistance' in an electric circuit?

options.text: The opposition to the flow of electric current through a material or component
The amount of charge that flows through a wire in one second of time
The energy supplied by a battery to push charges through the circuit
The brightness of a light bulb connected in a simple electrical circuit

quiz_questions.explanation: Resistance is the opposition to current flow. It depends on the material, length, and cross-sectional area of the conductor.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'voltage' or 'potential difference'?

options.text: The total amount of charge that passes a point in one second of time
The energy transferred per unit charge between two points in a circuit
The total resistance encountered by current in a complete circuit loop
The rate at which electrical energy is converted into heat energy

quiz_questions.explanation: Voltage (potential difference) is the energy transferred per unit charge between two points: V = W/q. It is measured in volts (V).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'resistivity' mean for a material?

options.text: A material property that measures how strongly it opposes electric current flow
The total resistance of a specific wire sample measured in an experiment
The voltage needed to push one ampere of current through a material
The temperature at which a material becomes a perfect conductor of current

quiz_questions.explanation: Resistivity (ρ) is an intrinsic property of a material that quantifies how strongly it resists current. It is measured in Ω·m and does not depend on the wire's dimensions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'ohmic' mean when describing a component?

options.text: The component has a resistance that changes depending on the current level
The component obeys Ohm's Law with constant resistance at fixed temperature
The component converts all electrical energy into thermal heat energy only
The component can only work with direct current and not alternating current

quiz_questions.explanation: An ohmic component has a constant resistance — the current is directly proportional to voltage (V = IR with constant R). Resistors at constant temperature are ohmic.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'electrical power' and what does it measure?

options.text: The rate at which electrical energy is transferred or converted in a circuit
The total amount of charge stored inside a battery before it runs out
The maximum voltage a power supply can deliver to an external circuit
The total resistance of all components connected in a circuit together

quiz_questions.explanation: Electrical power is the rate of energy transfer: P = IV = I²R = V²/R. It is measured in watts (W), where 1 W = 1 J/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'conventional current' in circuit analysis?

options.text: The flow of actual electrons from negative terminal to positive terminal
The assumed flow of positive charge from positive to negative terminal
The flow of neutrons through a metallic conductor carrying electricity
The oscillating current in an AC circuit that changes direction regularly

quiz_questions.explanation: Conventional current is the assumed direction of positive charge flow — from the positive terminal through the circuit to the negative terminal. This is opposite to actual electron flow.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'watt' (W) as a unit of power?

options.text: One joule of energy transferred every second through a circuit component
One coulomb of charge transferred every second through a circuit wire
One volt of potential difference applied across a one-ohm resistance
One ampere of current flowing through one meter of conducting wire

quiz_questions.explanation: One watt equals one joule of energy per second: 1 W = 1 J/s. It measures the rate of energy transfer in a circuit.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'electron flow' refer to in a circuit?

options.text: The movement of electrons from the positive terminal to negative terminal
The movement of electrons from the negative terminal to positive terminal
The movement of protons through a wire from one end to the other
The vibration of atoms in place without any actual charge movement

quiz_questions.explanation: Electron flow is the actual movement of electrons through a circuit — from the negative terminal of the battery through the external circuit to the positive terminal. This is opposite to conventional current.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'Ohm's Law' in simple terms?

options.text: Current through a conductor is proportional to voltage if resistance is constant
Resistance of a conductor increases when more current passes through it
Voltage across a conductor equals the power divided by the current flow
Power consumed by a resistor equals the voltage times the resistance value

quiz_questions.explanation: Ohm's Law states that the current through a conductor is directly proportional to the voltage across it, provided the resistance remains constant: V = IR.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'cross-sectional area' of a wire and how does it affect resistance?

options.text: The area of the wire's end face — larger area means higher resistance
The area of the wire's end face — larger area means lower resistance
The length of wire measured from one end to the other end entirely
The surface area of the wire's outer coating that insulates the wire

quiz_questions.explanation: Cross-sectional area is the area of the wire's circular end face. R = ρL/A shows that a larger area means lower resistance, as more electrons can flow simultaneously.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student connects a 10 Ω resistor to a 5 V battery. What current flows through the circuit?

options.text: The current is 50 A flowing through the circuit
The current is 2 A flowing through the circuit
The current is 0.5 A flowing through the circuit
The current is 15 A flowing through the circuit

quiz_questions.explanation: Using Ohm's Law: I = V/R = 5/10 = 0.5 A.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student measures 2 A of current flowing through a 6 Ω resistor. What is the voltage across it?

options.text: The voltage is 3 V across the resistor
The voltage is 12 V across the resistor
The voltage is 8 V across the resistor
The voltage is 4 V across the resistor

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices a light bulb glows dimmer when they add more resistors in series. Why?

options.text: The battery voltage decreases when more resistors are added to the circuit
Total resistance increases so less current flows through the entire circuit
The light bulb wears out more quickly when extra resistors are present
The wire connecting the components absorbs more electrical energy as heat

quiz_questions.explanation: Adding resistors in series increases total resistance. By Ohm's Law (I = V/R_total), higher resistance means less current, so the bulb receives less power and glows dimmer.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student calculates the power dissipated by a 100 Ω resistor with 0.5 A flowing through it. What is the power?

options.text: The power dissipated is 50 W of heat energy
The power dissipated is 25 W of heat energy
The power dissipated is 200 W of heat energy
The power dissipated is 100 W of heat energy

quiz_questions.question_text: A student replaces a thin wire with a thicker wire of the same material and length. What happens to the resistance?

options.text: The resistance increases because there is more material present
The resistance decreases because the cross-sectional area is larger
The resistance stays the same because the material has not changed
The resistance doubles because the wire diameter has increased

quiz_questions.explanation: R = ρL/A. A thicker wire has a larger cross-sectional area A. Since A is in the denominator, increasing it decreases resistance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student measures the current and voltage across a filament lamp as it heats up. They find the graph is curved, not straight. What does this tell them?

options.text: The lamp follows Ohm's Law because the graph shows a direct relationship
The lamp is non-ohmic because its resistance changes with temperature
The multimeter is broken because all components should give straight lines
The battery voltage is fluctuating and causing the curved graph shape

quiz_questions.explanation: A curved I-V graph means the resistance is not constant — it changes as the filament heats up. This makes the filament lamp a non-ohmic device. As temperature rises, resistance increases.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student connects a 12 V battery to a circuit and measures 3 A of current. What is the total resistance?

options.text: The total resistance is 36 Ω in the circuit
The total resistance is 4 Ω in the circuit
The total resistance is 15 Ω in the circuit
The total resistance is 9 Ω in the circuit

quiz_questions.question_text: A student wonders why the wires connecting components in a circuit don't get hot but the resistor does. Why?

options.text: Wires are made of insulating material that does not conduct electricity well
Wires have very low resistance so very little power is dissipated as heat
All the heat is absorbed by the resistor before it can reach the wires
The plastic insulation around the wires prevents them from heating at all

quiz_questions.explanation: P = I²R. The connecting wires are made of low-resistivity copper with large cross-sections, giving very low resistance. Since power dissipation depends on resistance, very little energy is converted to heat in the wires compared to the resistor.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices that a toaster uses thicker heating elements than a small LED circuit. Why do different devices use different wire thicknesses?

options.text: Thicker wires look better in larger household appliances than thin wires
Wire thickness is chosen based on the current it must carry safely without overheating
All wires in a circuit must have the same thickness for the circuit to function
Thicker wires are used only because they are cheaper than thinner wire types

quiz_questions.explanation: Higher current requires thicker wires (lower resistance) to prevent overheating from P = I²R. A toaster draws much more current than an LED, so it needs thicker wires to handle the current safely.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student measures 5 A flowing through a wire for 10 seconds. How much charge passes through the wire?

options.text: The charge that passes is 0.5 coulombs total
The charge that passes is 2 coulombs total
The charge that passes is 50 coulombs total
The charge that passes is 15 coulombs total

quiz_questions.explanation: Q = It = 5 A × 10 s = 50 C. Current is charge per unit time, so charge equals current times time.
